Supports of uterus

Pelvic floor support

Urinary and genital tracts have common interdependent support system

These supports are essential to hold the pelvic viscera and prevent them from vertical descent

Pelvic organ prolapse

Clinical note: The pelvic floor support is important in understanding pelvic organ prolapse (POP)

POP - herniation of the pelvic organs

Prolapse may involve uterus, vaginal cuff, bladder, small or large intestine (various combinations possible)

Delancey’s three levels of pelvic floor support

Structures supporting uterus and vagina are divided into three levels

Level 1 - Suspension

Level 2 - Attachment

Level 3 - Fusion

Level 1 – Suspension

Suspending structure attached to the lower part of the uterus - parametrium

Suspending structure attached to the vagina - paracolpium

Parametrium - is made of cardinal ligament / uterosacral ligament complex

Parametrium continues down as paracolpium

Level 1 structures suspend upper part of vagina and cervix from above

Uterosacral ligament complex

This is a part of continuous endopelvic fascia

Endopelvic fascia is a continuous system of sheaths over many pelvic viscera

Has collagen, elastin and smooth muscles

Endopelvic fascia has several condensations - called ‘ligaments’. These are fascial condensations and are not true ligaments.

Origin: in the region of S2 to S4 sacral vertebrae (with no bony attachment)

Insertion: into the dorsal aspect of uterine cervix and to the upper third of the vagina (posterior wall)

Components:

Superficial - covered by peritoneum

Deep - retroperitoneal

Relations - at the cervix - it is close to ureter

Cardinal ligament

Nomina anatomica term - parametrium

Unofficial terms - Tranverse cervical ligament / Mackenrodt ligament

Other terms - Uterosacral (sacrocervical) ligament

Structure: mesentery like structure covering the neurovascular elements from the internal iliac artery to the lateral aspect of the cervix and vagina

Anatomical existence of cardinal ligament is less defined. When the paracervical and pararectal spaces are opened up with blunt dissection, cardinal ligament (areolar tissue around the vessels) becomes more evident.

Clinical note: Uterine prolapse occurs when the cardinal-uterosacral ligament complex gets lax or breaks

Level 2 – Attachment

Middle third of vagina is supported by attachment of paracolpium between the vaginal wall and lateral pelvic wall

Forms arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis (ATFP) and fascia over the levator ani muscles

ATFP - arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis or white line

​​​​​​​Is the thickened condensation of the fascia over the iliococcygeus

Origin: ischial spine

Insertion: inner aspect of the pubic symphysis

It forms H shaped box like configuration around the distal vagina

Clinical note: Damage to the mid-level support of the vagina (level 2) results in cystocele and/or rectocele

Level 3 – Fusion

2-3 cm above the hymenal ring, vagina is fused in the following fashion

Laterally - levator ani muscles (urogenital diaphragm)

Posteriorly - perineal body

Anteriorly - blends with urethra

Superficially - perineal membrane with muscles of deep perineal space

Clinical note:

Anterior disruption of level 3 support results in urethral hypermobility and stress incontinence

Posterior disruption results in distal rectocele and perineal descent

Pelvic diaphragm (of pelvic floor)

Most important support of the pelvic viscera including the uterus

funnel-shaped fibromuscular partition in the pelvis

Composition:

Components of pelvic diaphragm

  1. Levator ani muscles
  2. Coccygeus muscles
  3. Superior and inferior fascia enclosing these muscles

It forms the roof of ischiorectal fossa

Levator ani

Broad, thin muscle at the pelvic floor

Critical role in pelvic support

Components

Pubococcygeus

  • Levator prostate in males and pubovaginalis in females

Puborectalis

Iliococcygeus

Pubococcygeus

Origin:

From the inner surface of the body of pelvis

From the tendinous arch of obturator fascia

Insertion:

Fibres from pubis are inserted into the perineal body

Fibres from the tendinous arch are inserted into the anococcygeal raphe and to the coccyx

Subdivisions

Anteriorly

In males - fibres around the prostate forms - levator prostate

In females - fibres around the vagina and cervix form pubovaginalis

Pubovaginalis - acts as vaginal sphincter

Posteriorly

U shaped sling around the rectum forms puborectalis

Puborectalis blends with the longitudinal fibres of the rectum

Puborectalis - creates anorectal angle. It is one of the important factors for the anal continence

Iliococcygeus

Origin:

From the posterior part of the tindinous arch

From the ischial spine (pelvic surface)

Insertion: into the anococcygeal raphe and to the sides of the coccyx

Ischiococcygeus

Origin: apex of ischial spine and sacrospinous ligament

Insertion: coccyx and to the S5 segment of sacrum

Sacrospious ligament - considered as degenerated tendon of the coccygeus

Kegel exercises

Voluntary contractions of all the perineal muscles

Strengthen all the striated muscles in the perineum

Uses -

  • Ameliorate erectile dysfunction due to venous leakage
  • Help men control premature ejaculation
  • Treat urinary incontinence in females

Ligaments of uterus and cervix

  • Broad ligament
  • Round ligament
  • Cardinal ligament
  • Uterosacral (sacrocervical) ligament
  • Anterior uterine (uterovesical) ligament
  • Posterior uterine (rectovaginal) ligament
  • Pubocervical fascia
  • Utero-ovarian ligament
  • Rectouterine ligament

Broad ligament

It is the mesentery of the uterus, tubes, and ovaries

It is the large peritoneal fold enclosing parametrium

Extends from body of uterus to the lateral pelvic wall

Compartments of pelvis

Uterus with broad ligament on either side divides the pelvis into two parts

  • Anterior or vesicouterine fossa
  • Posterior or rectouterine fossa (cul-de-sac of Douglas)

Broad ligaments are of no mechanical support to the uterus and cervix

Borders of broad ligament

  • Medial border - lateral edge of uterus, encloses uterine vessels
  • Lateral border - related to lateral pelvic wall
  • Superior border - envelops the uterine tube, vessels, ovarian ligament and round ligament (anteriorly)
  • Inferior border - extends over the floor of the true pelvis

Other named parts of the broad ligament

Mesovarium - posterior layer of the broad ligament attached to the anterior margin of the ovary

  • It has ovarian blood vessels and nerves

Mesosalphix - part of the broad ligament between the uterine tube and ovary

  • It has uterine tube, round ligament, ligament of ovary, paraoophoron and epoopharon

Suspensory ligament of ovary - part lateral to ovary

  • It has ovarian blood vessels and nerves

Mesometrium - part of the broad ligament below the mesovarium

Parametrium - thick connective lateral to the lower uterine segment and cervix

Round ligament

Remnant of gubernaculum overii

Arise from - upper part of the lateral aspect of the body (at the junction of the uterine tubes)

Course

Travel anteriorly and laterally through mesometrium

Leaves pelvis through deep inguinal ring

Passes though the inguinal canal

Terminates - near mons pubis

Pubocervical fascia

Thick anterior connective tissue in relation to the cervix and vagina where urethra is embedded

Utero-ovarian ligament or ovarian ligament

Derived from gubernaculum ovarii

Attaches the ovary to the body of the uterus

Rectouterine ligament

Extends between the pericervical ring to the upper vagina

Broad ligament, mesovarium, mesosalphix and round ligament do not play a role in support of pelvic organs

Clinical importance - Pelvic Organ Prolapse

​​​​​​​Uterovaginal prolapse - prolapse of the uterus, cervix and upper vagina

Urethrocele - Prolapse of the lower anterior vaginal wall involving the urethra only

Cystocele - Prolapse of the upper anterior vaginal wall involving the bladder

Cystourethrocele - prolapse of the urethra and bladder

Enterocele - Prolapse of the upper posterior wall of the vagina usually containing loops of small bowel

Rectocele - Prolapse of the lower posterior wall of the vagina involving the rectum bulging forwards into the vagina

Clinical importance - Grading of POP

Grade 0 – no prolapse

Grade 1–halfway to hymen

Grade 2 – to hymen

Grade 3 – halfway past hymen

Grade 4 –maximum descent
